event: The Venus of Dolní Věstonice, one of the world's oldest known ceramic figurines, is created by Gravettian mammoth hunters in Moravia.
Neolithic farmers of the Linear Pottery culture settle the fertile lowlands of Bohemia and Moravia, introducing agriculture.
Celtic tribes, including the Boii who give Bohemia its name, dominate the region during the La Tène period.
Germanic tribes, notably the Marcomanni under Maroboduus, displace the Celtic Boii and establish a powerful kingdom in Bohemia.
Rome plans a campaign against Maroboduus's Marcomannic kingdom but abandons it due to a revolt in Pannonia.
Emperor Marcus Aurelius concludes the Marcomannic Wars, fought partly on the soil of present-day Czech lands.
Slavic tribes begin migrating into Bohemia and Moravia, gradually becoming the dominant population.
Samo unites Slavic tribes against the Avars, founding Samo's Empire, the first known Slavic political entity in the region.
Prince Mojmír I founds Great Moravia, the first major West Slavic state centered in Moravia.
Byzantine missionaries Cyril and Methodius arrive in Great Moravia, bringing the Glagolitic alphabet and Slavic liturgy.
Great Moravia collapses under Magyar invasions, shifting power toward the emerging Bohemian principality.
Duke Wenceslas I, later Saint Wenceslas and patron of the Czechs, is murdered by his brother Boleslav I.
The Bishopric of Prague is established, anchoring the Czech lands within Latin Christendom.
Vratislaus II becomes the first King of Bohemia, granted the royal title by Emperor Henry IV.
The Golden Bull of Sicily confirms hereditary kingship for the Přemyslid dynasty and Bohemia's autonomy within the Empire.
The assassination of Wenceslas III ends the Přemyslid dynasty, opening the throne to the Luxembourg dynasty.
Charles IV founds Charles University in Prague, the first university in Central Europe, during Prague's golden age.
Charles IV is crowned Holy Roman Emperor, making Prague the imperial capital and a leading European cultural center.
Religious reformer Jan Hus is burned at the stake at the Council of Constance, sparking the Hussite movement.
The First Defenestration of Prague ignites the Hussite Wars against the Catholic Church and Empire.
Ferdinand I of Habsburg is elected King of Bohemia, beginning nearly four centuries of Habsburg rule.
The Second Defenestration of Prague triggers the Bohemian Revolt and the wider Thirty Years' War.
Bohemian Protestant forces are crushed at the Battle of White Mountain, leading to harsh Habsburg recatholicization.
The Peace of Westphalia ends the Thirty Years' War, leaving the Czech lands devastated and firmly under Habsburg control.
Maria Theresa accedes to the Habsburg throne, beginning Enlightenment reforms across Bohemia and Moravia.
Emperor Joseph II abolishes serfdom and issues the Patent of Toleration, modernizing the Czech lands.
Revolutions sweep the Habsburg Empire as Czech national revival leaders convene the Prague Slavic Congress.
The Austro-Hungarian Compromise leaves the Czech lands within Austria, fueling Czech demands for autonomy.
Czechoslovakia is proclaimed an independent republic on 28 October as Austria-Hungary collapses after World War I.
Edvard Beneš succeeds Tomáš Garrigue Masaryk as president of democratic Czechoslovakia.
The Munich Agreement forces Czechoslovakia to cede the Sudetenland to Nazi Germany without its consent.
Nazi Germany occupies the Czech lands, establishing the Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia.
Reinhard Heydrich is assassinated in Prague, prompting brutal Nazi reprisals including the destruction of Lidice.
Soviet and American troops liberate Czechoslovakia as World War II ends and the ethnic Germans are expelled.
The Communist Party seizes full power in a coup, making Czechoslovakia a Soviet-aligned one-party state.
The Prague Spring reforms are crushed when Warsaw Pact forces invade to halt Alexander Dubček's liberalization.
Student Jan Palach self-immolates in Prague in protest against the Soviet occupation.
Dissidents publish Charter 77, a human-rights manifesto that becomes a focus of opposition to communist rule.
The peaceful Velvet Revolution topples the communist regime, bringing Václav Havel to the presidency.
Czechoslovakia peacefully splits in the Velvet Divorce, creating the independent Czech Republic on 1 January.
The Czech Republic joins the United Nations as a sovereign state.
The Czech Republic joins NATO, anchoring itself in the Western security alliance.
The Czech Republic becomes a member of the European Union in the bloc's largest enlargement.
The Czech Republic joins the Schengen Area, abolishing border controls with neighboring states.
Miloš Zeman becomes the first Czech president elected by direct popular vote.
The country adopts 'Czechia' as its official short geographic name in English.
Former NATO general Petr Pavel is elected president, succeeding Miloš Zeman.
